title: Norway's Oil Output Beats Forecasts as Hormuz Traffic Stays Near Crisis Lows | OilPrice.com
text:
India Pulls Back From Iraqi Oil as Hormuz Turns Too Dangerous

Jan-Thore is a Norwegian oil and gas expert with two decades of industry experience across a variety of roles. For the past five years, he…

Norway's crude oil production beat official forecasts in June as offshore fields returned from seasonal maintenance, delivering a stronger-than-expected increase in supply at a time when global markets remain focused on disruptions to Middle Eastern exports.

According to preliminary figures released Tuesday by the Norwegian Offshore Directorate (SODIR), Norway's crude oil production averaged 1.827 million barrels per day (bpd) in June, exceeding the agency's official forecast while rising sharply from 1.677 million bpd in the same month last year.

The stronger-than-expected output reflects the return of key fields from seasonal maintenance and continued strong operational performance across the Norwegian Continental Shelf, reinforcing Norway's position as one of Europe's most reliable oil suppliers.

Including natural gas liquids (NGL) and condensate, Norway's total liquids production reached 2.022 million bpd, up from 1.909 million bpd in May, marking a month-on-month increase of approximately 113,000 bpd.

Natural gas production also recovered after seasonal maintenance. Gas sales totaled 10.0 billion standard cubic meters (Sm³) in June, up from 9.4 billion Sm³ in May, as Norwegian exports continued to underpin Europe's energy security following the sharp decline in Russian pipeline gas deliveries.

The rebound in Norwegian production comes as global oil markets continue to monitor shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz. Reuters, citing Kpler vessel-tracking data, reported that tanker traffic through the strategic waterway remains close to the depressed levels seen during the height of the recent conflict, underscoring persistent risks to global oil and gas flows from the Persian Gulf. Against that backdrop, additional barrels from reliable producers such as Norway provide a welcome source of stability for European energy markets.

While Norway accounts for only around 2% of global crude oil production, its importance to Europe is disproportionately large. As the continent's largest supplier of natural gas and a key exporter of crude oil, stable production from the Norwegian Continental Shelf has become an increasingly important pillar of Europe's energy security.

Brent crude has retreated sharply from the highs reached during the Strait of Hormuz crisis and has largely returned to pre-crisis price levels. Even so, uncertainty surrounding Middle Eastern export flows continues to support a geopolitical risk premium, while commercial crude inventories in several major consuming regions remain relatively tight.

Norway's ability to restore production quickly following planned maintenance underscores the operational reliability of the Norwegian Continental Shelf. Production from giant fields such as Johan Sverdrup, Troll and Johan Castberg continues to provide Europe with a dependable source of crude oil and natural gas amid an increasingly uncertain geopolitical environment.

The strong year-on-year increase also reflects the continued ramp-up and optimization of recently developed projects together with high operational uptime across several of Norway's largest producing assets. While seasonal maintenance will continue to create month-to-month fluctuations, Norway remains one of the world's most stable and predictable non-OPEC producers.

While OPEC+ is set to raise production targets by another 188,000 barrels per day in August, the group's ability to translate higher quotas into additional exports remains clouded by continued logistical disruptions in the Persian Gulf. Reuters, citing Kpler vessel-tracking data, reported that only a handful of commodity vessels crossed the Strait of Hormuz on a recent day, with no VLCCs or LNG tankers recorded. Against that backdrop, stronger-than-expected Norwegian production provides additional barrels from one of the world's most reliable non-OPEC producers at a time when energy security remains firmly in focus.
